query: evidence on thiotepa use in osteosarcoma treatment

retrieved_docs.title: Telangiectatic Osteosarcoma(Archived) -- Treatment Planning
retrieved_docs.content: Chemotherapeutic protocols for telangiectatic osteosarcoma are similar to those of conventional osteosarcoma. Patients will have two to six cycles of chemotherapy administered before surgery. The common chemotherapeutic agents used include cisplatin, carboplatin, methotrexate, doxorubicin, and ifosfamide. [25] Among these, at least two agents need inclusion in a dual-drug therapy regimen. The effect of chemotherapy on telangiectatic osteosarcoma must undergo reassessment. The percentage of tumor necrosis needs to be assessed in the pathological evaluation of the surgical specimen, as <90% necrosis mandates a change in the chemotherapeutic regimen. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma(Archived) -- Medical Oncology -- National Comprehensive Cancer Network's 2020 Recommendations for Chemotherapy Agents & Regimens for Treatment of Osteosarcoma (Version 1.2020)
retrieved_docs.content: Recommended Neoadjuvant/Adjuvant Chemotherapy Regimens for Initial-Occurrence Cisplatin and doxorubicin (Category 1) MAP (high-dose methotrexate, cisplatin, and doxorubicin) (Category 1) Doxorubicin, cisplatin, ifosfamide, and high-dose methotrexate Recommended Chemotherapy Regimens for Relapsed, Refractory or Metastatic Disease Regorafenib (Category 1) Ifosfamide (high dose) +/- etoposide Sorafenib Sorafenib and everolimus

retrieved_docs.title: Anthracyclines -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Anthracyclines are drugs extracted from Streptomyces spp. used in the treatment of various types of cancers. The different types available for treatment are: Daunorubicin Doxorubicin Epirubicin Idarubicin Mitoxantrone Valrubicin Anthracyclines are among the most effective chemotherapy agents and cover a significant number of cancers. [1] FDA Approved Indications [2] [3] [4] Acute lymphocytic leukemia Acute myelogenous leukemia Hodgkin's lymphoma Non-Hodgkin's lymphoma Bladder cancer Rapidly recurrent (Ta or Ti) or in situ transitional cell carcinoma (Intravesical therapy) Neoadjuvant treatment Metastatic transitional cell bladder cancer Breast cancer Adjuvant therapy following axillary lymph node resection in primary breast cancer Neoadjuvant therapy Metastatic breast cancer Other metastatic cancers Ovarian cancer Osteogenic sarcoma Ewing sarcoma Soft tissue sarcoma Thyroid cancer Neuroblastoma Wilm's tumor Small cell lung cancer

retrieved_docs.title: Sarcoma -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: The use of systemic chemotherapy in treating non-pediatric extremity and trunk sarcomas has been studied extensively but has suffered from low enrollment and inconsistent results, making it difficult to determine the overall benefit to patients. Most conventional chemotherapy regimens are anthracycline-based as they appear to be the most active agents in sarcoma patients. Systemic therapy can be utilized in neoadjuvant, adjuvant, metastatic, and recurrent settings. [35] The most common adjuvant/neoadjuvant regimens are AIM (Doxorubicin/Ifosphamide/Mesna) and gemcitabine/docetaxel. Unfortunately, randomized trials to date have failed to demonstrate a disease-free or overall survival with conventional anthracycline-based chemotherapy regimens in both the adjuvant and neoadjuvant settings. [46] [47] However, a large meta-analysis of adjuvant chemotherapy did show improvement in local and distant recurrence and a nonsignificant improvement in overall survival. [48] It is thought that the overall benefit of chemotherapy is likely small. [48] For advanced and metastatic STS, anthracyclines are the first-line therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Ifosfamide -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Ewing sarcoma: According to the American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) guidelines for recurrent or refractory Ewing sarcoma, high-dose ifosfamide proves to be more effective in extending survival compared to the topotecan and cyclophosphamide regimen . [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma(Archived) -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: “Category 1” recommendations are those based upon high-level evidence, with uniform NCCN consensus that the intervention is appropriate. This designation represents the highest level of clinical confidence in efficacy.

retrieved_docs.title: Ifosfamide -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical cancer—recurrent or metastatic : Ifosfamide is administered alongside mesna at 1500 g/m²/d every 3 weeks. The response rate ranges from 18% to 45% when combined with paclitaxel and cisplatin. [7] Bladder carcinoma: Bladder carcinoma is typically treated with ifosfamide in cases of advanced bladder cancer. [8] Non-Hodgkin lymphoma: Ifosfamide is salvage therapy in Burkitt lymphoma and diffuses large B-cell lymphoma. Hodgkin lymphoma: In relapsed or refractory cases, ifosfamide is administered as part of the RICE regimen (rituximab, ifosfamide, carboplatin, and etoposide) along with mesna. [9] Small-cell and non-small-cell lung cancer: Studies have shown that ifosfamide provides more benefits than standard regimens when used as maintenance or consolidation therapy. [10] Osteosarcoma: High-dose ifosfamide proves to be an effective, standalone treatment for patients with refractory or recurrent osteosarcoma. [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Telangiectatic Osteosarcoma(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Previously, the prognosis and clinical outcome of telangiectatic osteosarcoma were considered to be poor. However, with the advent of chemotherapy, the prognosis has significantly improved, with around a 60% survival rate at ten years, which is similar to that of conventional osteosarcoma. [22] Patients with telangiectatic osteosarcoma receive neo-adjuvant chemotherapy followed by surgical excision, further followed by adjuvant chemotherapy/ radiotherapy. The pre-chemotherapy era required amputation in most cases of telangiectatic osteosarcoma. However, limb-sparing surgery is now possible in most cases, even with pathological fractures. [23] [22] [24] The extent of surgery depends on the size, location, and response to the neoadjuvant therapy. Adjuvant chemotherapy can offer some advantages if metastatic disease is present.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma (Osteogenic Sarcoma) -- Medical Oncology -- Relapsed or Refractory Disease Regimens
retrieved_docs.content: Regimens recommended for osteosarcoma relapse or refractory disease include: Etoposide plus ifosfamide (most commonly used second-line regimen) Alternative options Regorafenib High dose ifosfamide +/- etoposide Sorafenib +/- everolimus Cyclophosphamide and topotecan Docetaxel and gemcitabine Gemcitabine alone The following combinatorial regimens may be useful in specific circumstances: Cyclophosphamide and etoposide Ifosfamide, carboplatin, and etoposide HD-MTX, etoposide, and ifosfamide [57] [NCCN Guidelines Bone Cancer] [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma (Osteogenic Sarcoma) -- Medical Oncology -- Chemotherapy for Osteosarcoma
retrieved_docs.content: Chemotherapy is also the standard of care for high-grade osteogenic sarcoma, given in the neoadjuvant and adjuvant setting. However, the ideal timing of chemotherapy (ie, preoperative versus postoperative) is unclear.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma(Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- National Comprehensive Cancer Network's 2020 Guidelines for Management of Osteosarcoma (Version 1.2020)
retrieved_docs.content: If a relapse is detected, the following are the guidelines to follow: Chemotherapy +/- resection (if possible) Response to these treatments should have an evaluation via: Radiographs of the original tumor site CT or MRI (both with contrast) of the site of relapse CT of the chest to assess for pulmonary lesions Good response to treatment: Surveillance (restart OSTEO-4 guidelines) Poor response/progression of the disease: Resection (if possible) Clinical trial Palliative radiation Best supportive care

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma(Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Extraskeletal Osteosarcoma
retrieved_docs.content: Follow the National Comprehensive Cancer Network's guidelines for the treatment of soft tissue sarcoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma(Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- National Comprehensive Cancer Network's 2020 Guidelines for Management of Osteosarcoma (Version 1.2020)
retrieved_docs.content: OSTEO-1 (Low-Grade Osteosarcoma, No Metastasis) Intramedullary and surface Wide excision alone (no neoadjuvant chemotherapy) If postsurgical pathology demonstrates low-grade features, then no adjuvant chemotherapy If postsurgical pathology demonstrates high-grade features, consider adjuvant chemotherapy Periosteal Neoadjuvant chemotherapy then perform a wide excision If postsurgical pathology demonstrates is consistent with biopsy (low grade features only) then no adjuvant chemotherapy If postsurgical pathology demonstrates high-grade features, consider adjuvant chemotherapy

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma (Osteogenic Sarcoma) -- Treatment / Management -- Supportive Management and Palliative Medicine
retrieved_docs.content: The management of chemotherapy-related complications such as nausea and vomiting, anemia, neutropenic fever, fatigue, neuropathy, and cardiotoxicity, provision of symptom-directed therapy, and counseling regarding goals of care discussions have shown improvement in the quality of life. The provision of continuity of care through home care and round-the-clock telephonic liaison might assume special significance, depending on environmental circumstances (eg, the COVID-19 pandemic). Hospice should be considered early. [39] [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Ifosfamide -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Soft tissue sarcomas: Studies have shown that combination therapy with ifosfamide and mesna demonstrates objective response rates of around 40% when used as induction therapy. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: BCG vaccine is classified as an antitumor substance and, together with other antitumor substances, belongs to the therapeutic class cortisone acetate. Cortisone acetate is the active ingredient of complex oral liquid formulations such as Bio Tonsilla Phase, which also contain SOLANUM DULCAMARA, ECHINACEA ANGUSTIFOLIA, HORSE CHESTNUT, ANTIMONY POTASSIUM TARTRATE, ASCORBIC ACID and various tissue‑derived extracts. A broad spectrum of agents—including corticosteroids (cortisol acetate, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone sodium phosphate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasone sonikotinát, prednisolone acetate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, prednisolone tebutate), antimetabolites (cytarabine, methotrexate sodium, hydroxy‑mocovina/hydroxyurea, leucovorin, azacytidine, aminopterin), alkylating agents (busulfan, hexamethylmelamin, pipobroman, mitobronitol, mitotan, nafoxidine, oxid arsenitý, pentostatin), mitotic inhibitors (vincristine sulfate, estramustin, mitoguazon), topoisomerase inhibitors (irinotecan, irinotecan hydrochloride) and biologic or viral therapies (talimogene laherparevvec, human‑herpesvirus‑1‑based therapy) are reported to may_treat leukemia. Talimogene laherparevvec is specifically contraindicated in patients with leukemia, and the presence of human‑herpesvirus‑1 infection is a contraindication for leukemia treatment. Within the ATC system, antitumor substances constitute a subclass of “other antineoplastic agents” and include drugs such as dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone sodium phosphate, anagrelide, aminopterin, irinotecan and azacytidine; anagrelide exemplifies phosphodiesterase inhibition as its anticancer mechanism. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that antitumor agents span classic chemotherapeutics, targeted and viral immunotherapies, and adjunctive corticosteroids, all integrated to treat leukemia while accounting for specific contraindications such as HSV‑1 infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1554
retrieved_docs.content: Dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, methotrexate sodium, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium phosphate are therapeutic agents that may be employed in the treatment of ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم. The condition ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم is classified as a leukemia, indicating a malignant disorder of the blood‑forming tissues. In contrast, talimogene laherparepvec is contraindicated for use in patients with ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم, reflecting a restriction rather than a therapeutic role. These relationships highlight the range of pharmacologic options for managing this hematologic malignancy and the importance of recognizing agents that should be avoided.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2712
retrieved_docs.content: Alkylating agents constitute a class of chemotherapeutic drugs that exert their antineoplastic effect by adding alkyl groups to DNA, causing cross‑linking and ultimately cell death. Mitomycin is an example of an alkylating agent, illustrating the typical properties of this drug class. Because agents within this class share similar mechanisms of action and overlapping toxicities, the concurrent use of cyclophosphamide—a drug also belonging to the alkylating agent category—with other alkylating agents is generally contraindicated. This contraindication reflects the need to avoid additive adverse effects, such as heightened bone‑marrow suppression and organ toxicity, when multiple alkylating agents are administered together. Clinicians therefore carefully consider drug selection and avoid combining cyclophosphamide with other alkylating agents like mitomycin to ensure safer patient care.

Leukemia is managed with a broad spectrum of antineoplastic agents that belong to the ATC class “other antineoplastic agents.” These include corticosteroid formulations (dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone sodium phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, prednisolone tebutate), nucleoside analogues (cytarabine, floxuridine, fluorouracil), topoisomerase I inhibitors (irinotecan, irinotecan hydrochloride), antimetabolites and folate pathway inhibitors (hydroxyurea, methotrexate sodium, aminopterin, leucovorin/folinic acid, mitobronitol), alkylating/cross‑linking agents (hexamethylmelamine, busulfan, pipobroman, poly A‑U, nafazatrom), hormonal/endocrine agents (nafoxidine, mitotane), phosphodiesterase inhibitor anagrelide, and other diverse compounds (estramustin, kyselina nordihydroguaiaretová, mitoguazon, oxid arsenitý, pentostatin, azacytidine, anagrelide,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 64
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibitors” mode‑of‑action class. The higher‑level antitumor agents class includes many of the same compounds as children: asparaginase, azacitidine, busulfan, cytarabine, floxuridine, fluorouracil, hexamethylmelamine, aminopterin, hydroxymočovina (hydroxyurea), leukovorin (folinic acid), mitobronitol, mitotan, nafoxidin, pipobroman, poly A‑U, and vincristine sulfate. This hierarchy demonstrates that numerous drugs listed under “other antineoplastic agents” are also part of the larger antitumor agents family, sharing the common therapeutic purpose of inhibiting tumor growth or inducing tumor cell death. In addition to traditional chemotherapeutics, the antitumor agents group encompasses antiviral oncolytic therapies (human herpesvirus 1, talimogene laherparevec) and glucocorticoid steroids used in oncology (dexamethasone phosphate and the prednisolone esters listed above). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: BCG vakcína is classified as an antitumor agent and belongs to the therapeutic class antitumorózní látky, which is a subset of the ATC group “other antineoplastic agents.” Antitumorózní látky comprise a broad spectrum of oncology drugs, including nucleoside analogues (floxuridin, fluorouracil, azacytidin/azacitidine, cytarabin/cytarabine), classic chemotherapeutics (irinotecan, irinotecan hydrochlorid, methotrexate sodium, busulfan, aminopterin, pentostatin, hexamethylmelamin, hydroxymočovina, mitobronitol, mitotan, nafoxidin, pipobroman, poly A‑U, leukovorin), targeted agents (anagrelide, estramustin, kyselina nordihydroguaiaretová, mitoguazon, oxid arsenitý/arsenic trioxide), and oncolytic viral therapies (talimogene laherparevec, lidský herpesvirus 1, which is also used in oncologic immunotherapy). The class also contains several corticosteroid derivatives employed in cancer care, such as kortizon acetát (cortisol acetate), dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, prednisolone acetate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate; oral liquid formulations (e.g., Bio Tonsilla Phase) list kortizon acetát as the active ingredient. All of these agents are reported to may_treat ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم, an Arabic‑named form of leukemia. In this disease context, prednisolone tebutate is indicated as a possible therapy, whereas talimogene laherparevec is specifically contraindicated. Additionally, infection with lidský herpesvirus 1 (human herpesvirus 1) is contraindicated in patients with this leukemia. Collectively, the network links BCG‑related antitumor activity, a hierarchical taxonomy of antineoplastic and corticosteroid drugs, and therapeutic decision‑making for leukemia, emphasizing both effective treatment options and critical contraindications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 40
retrieved_docs.content: BCG vaccine is classified as an antitumor agent within the broader antineoplastic therapy group. Cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasone sodium phosphate, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate are identified as drugs used to treat leukemias, with dexamethasone acetate and dexamethasone sodium phosphate also described as antitumor agents employed in septic shock management. Human herpesvirus‑1 infection and the oncolytic virus therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with leukemia, highlighting important safety considerations. In the ATC classification, antitumor agents serve as a parent category that includes “other antineoplastic agents,” encompassing drugs such as anagrelide, estramustin, hexamethylmelamin, hydroxymočovina, kyselina nordihydroguaiaretová, mitoguazon, mitotan, oxid arsenitý, and pentostatin.
